title: HTML Text Formatting: Semantic Tags & Best Practices (Complete Guide)

meta.title: HTML Formatting Tags – Bold, Italic, Semantic Emphasis & More (2026)
meta.description: Complete guide to HTML text formatting tags: bold (<b>, <strong>), italic (<i>, <em>), underline, mark, code, sub/sup, del/ins, and semantic best practices for accessibility.

content.value: What is HTML Text Formatting?

HTML text formatting refers to using special tags to control the appearance and meaning of text content. Unlike CSS (which handles **visual styling**), HTML formatting tags often carry **semantic meaning** — they tell browsers, search engines, and screen readers *why* text looks a certain way, not just *how* it looks.
Quick Reference: All Formatting Tags
Semantic vs Presentational: The Critical Distinction
Understanding the difference between **semantic** (meaningful) and **presentational** (visual-only) tags is crucial for writing accessible, SEO-friendly HTML.

rows: `<strong>` for importance (alerts, warnings). `<b>` only for visual bold without meaning (product names).
`<em>` for verbal stress. `<i>` for foreign words, technical terms, or thoughts.
`<u>` only for misspellings. Use CSS `text-decoration` for underlines.

Accessibility Considerations

value: **Screen readers announce `<strong>` and `<em>`** with altered tone/emphasis — use them intentionally
**`<b>` and `<i>` receive no special announcement** — purely visual
**Don't use `<u>` for links** — users expect underlined text to be clickable (use CSS for link styling)
**`<mark>` helps users find search keywords** — screen readers announce "highlighted"
**`<abbr>` with `title` provides expansion** — essential for accessibility
**Never rely on visual formatting alone** to convey meaning (color, bold, italics must have text alternatives)

content.value: Best Practices Summary

value: ✅ **Use `<b>` and `<i>` sparingly** — only when no semantic meaning is needed
✅ **Use `<mark>` to highlight relevant text** (search results, quotes)
✅ **Use `<del>` and `<ins>` for document revisions** (not for styling)
✅ **Use `<abbr>` for all abbreviations** on first occurrence
✅ **Use `<cite>` for creative work titles** (books, articles, movies)
❌ **Don't use formatting tags just for visual effect** — use CSS instead

content.value: Common Mistakes & Fixes

rows: Use CSS `text-decoration: underline` or `<ins>` if appropriate
Always close tags in reverse order

content.value: CSS Alternative: When to Use CSS Instead
For purely visual effects that carry no meaning, use CSS instead of HTML formatting tags.
Conclusion

HTML formatting tags go far beyond making text bold or italic. They provide semantic meaning that improves accessibility, SEO, and code maintainability. Use `<strong>` and `<em>` for meaning, `<b>` and `<i>` only when necessary, and leverage technical tags like `<code>`, `<kbd>`, and `<abbr>` for richer content. When in doubt, ask: *"Does this formatting carry meaning, or is it just visual?"* — if it's just visual, use CSS.

faq.question: Should I use <b> or <strong>?
faq.answer: Use `<strong>` when the text has importance (warnings, key points). Use `<b>` only for visual bold without meaning (product names, keywords without emphasis).

faq.question: What's the difference between <em> and <i>?
faq.answer: `<em>` indicates stress emphasis ("I *really* mean it") — screen readers change tone. `<i>` is for foreign words, thoughts, or technical terms without added emphasis.

faq.question: Can I nest formatting tags?
faq.answer: Yes — `Important and emphasized` is valid. Screen readers will announce both.

faq.question: Does <mark> affect SEO?
faq.answer: Indirectly. `<mark>` highlights relevant content (like search keywords). Google may consider marked text as relevant to the user's query.

faq.question: When should I use <code> vs <pre>?
faq.answer: `<code>` is for **inline** code snippets. `<pre>` preserves whitespace/indentation. Use `<pre><code>` together for multi-line code blocks.

faq.question: Is <u> deprecated?
faq.answer: No, but repurposed. In HTML5, `<u>` represents text with a non-textual annotation (misspellings, proper names in Chinese). Don't use it for underlining links or decorative underlines.
